premise: A disgraced war general and the fae spy sent to kill her must survive each other long enough to stop a god from returning.
fantasySubgenreNotes: Epic fantasy foundation but with a subversive/unconventional twist — not standard chosen-one heroics. The god isn't evil, the empire isn't noble, the spy isn't villainous. Moral ambiguity baked into the structure.

moodAndTone.notes: Lean into the assassination subtext as ongoing romantic and dramatic tension. No clean heroes. The spy's kill order doesn't go away — it is a live wire throughout. Trust is structural failure, not comfort. Twilight-era atmosphere: not ruins, but thinning confidence.

abstractPatterns: Emotionally restrained prose — reveals through omission and gesture, not declaration
Short declarative sentences as default rhythm, with occasional longer reflective passages
Dry, understated humor in dialogue — characters deflect with wit under pressure
Internal monologue that shows what characters notice and avoid, not what they feel directly
Character dynamics conveyed through small physical actions (squeezing a hand, crossing arms, catching a dagger)
Scene transitions via hard cuts (+++), not bridging narration
Comfort with silence and white space — not every emotion needs a sentence
Ensemble dialogue that differentiates voices through cadence and preoccupation, not dialect
styleReference.safetyNote: Abstract stylistic patterns only — no content, phrases, metaphors, or plot elements reproduced (INV-023, INV-024)

worldSeed.nameHandling: ALL names from the world seed must be replaced with original names. This includes character names, place names, organization names, magical terms, and proper nouns. The structural and thematic concepts are source material; the specific nomenclature is not.
coreConcepts: The Veil — atmospheric phenomenon that is the skin/remains of a defeated god, dimming starlight and muffling magic, now tearing
Imprisoned god — not evil, but incompatible with a finished world; god of unmade things and unrealized potential
The general — disgraced after a battlefield call that was right but inconvenient; lives in internal exile; meticulous, ruthless in crisis, never asks for help
Loyalty Brand — magic-suppressing brand on the general, supposedly temporary but being secretly refreshed; slowly killing her
The fae spy — from a fae intelligence network; sent to kill the general because she's the only person who knows the location of the god-seal mechanism
Kill order has a binding clause from the fae court — cannot return home without completing it
The god-seal location exists only as embodied memory in the general — a route she walked once, blindfolded, that she only knows when she moves
Someone on the Imperial Council has been poisoning the Veil for forty years
Tonal framework: twilight-era empire with thinning confidence; fae are bureaucratic and cold, not whimsical; the god bleeds through as wrongness in the natural world
Romance built on antagonistic competence — grudging recognition of skill; trust as structural failure

constraints.writingStyle: Must follow abstract stylistic patterns extracted from external-resources/writing-style.txt
constraints.moralComplexity: No clean heroes. The assassination dynamic remains a live tension throughout — not resolved by the spy simply deciding not to do it.
themes: The cost of duty vs. desire
Whether the 'right' choice and the 'good' choice are the same thing
What it means to be someone's only option vs. their choice

appliedDefaults: characterSeeds: none — pipeline generates from scratch using world seed context
